subsystem: PHP ext/session RFC 1867 (multipart/form-data) upload-progress callback. php_session_rfc1867_callback is the hook the SAPI multipart parser invokes for each stage of a file upload (START, per-field FORMDATA, per-file FILE_START/FILE_DATA/FILE_END, END). It maintains a per-request php_session_rfc1867_progress object that mirrors upload state into $_SESSION["PHP_SESSION_UPLOAD_PROGRESS"] so scripts can poll progress.
provenance: Identified by the function name php_session_rfc1867_callback and the PS(...) accessor macro (PHP's per-thread session globals accessor), the multipart_event_* event structs and MULTIPART_EVENT_* enum, the zval/ZVAL_STRINGL/zval_dtor/zend_string_init/zend_hash_str_find/smart_str_* Zend engine API, and ecalloc/efree (PHP's emalloc allocator). The header comment 'primevul_c_train_3258_fixed' marks it as a PrimeVul-derived sample presented as the fixed variant. This is the RFC 1867 upload-progress code from ext/session/session.c.
trust_boundary: The whole function runs on an attacker-controlled request body: a remote client sends a multipart/form-data POST and fully controls the number, order, names, sizes and contents of parts. event_data is a union of multipart_event_* structs populated by the multipart parser from wire bytes; data->name, *data->value, *data->filename, data->length, *data->newlength, data->content_length, data->offset and data->post_bytes_processed are all attacker-influenced. The orig callback (line 7-9) is external and, per the line-33 comment, may have rewritten *data->newlength before this code runs. The event ordering is produced by the SAPI parser; this code assumes MULTIPART_EVENT_START fires first and creates the progress object that every later branch dereferences.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. 'event' is a stage selector (an unsigned int matching the MULTIPART_EVENT_* enum), 'event_data' is an opaque pointer cast per-case to a specific multipart_event_* struct, and 'extra' is passed straight through to a chained callback. If a previous handler was registered it is called first (line 7-9) and its return value seeds retval. If upload-progress tracking is disabled the function returns immediately. Otherwise it loads the current per-request progress object from the session globals: progress = PS(rfc1867_progress). Critically, this pointer is only ever populated by the MULTIPART_EVENT_START case below, and is NULL both before START runs and after END frees it — nothing on this path validates it before the switch.

The START case allocates the progress object with ecalloc (PHP's zeroing emalloc; it does not return NULL — it aborts the request on allocation failure, so no NULL check is required here). It records the request's declared content_length (from the Content-Length header, attacker-controlled) and precomputes the length of the configured session name. It stores the object back into PS(rfc1867_progress) so later events can find it. This is the ONLY producer of the progress object; every other case is a pure consumer.

For each non-file form field the parser fires FORMDATA. Line 29 short-circuits if the session id and key are already resolved (both found on an earlier field) — but this test itself dereferences progress (progress->sid, progress->key.s). Lines 33-38 choose the value length: if a chained callback set *data->newlength (the comment explicitly warns it may have been modified) that overrides the wire length data->length. value_len then governs how many bytes are copied out of *data->value below.

If the field name equals the configured session name, its value is stored as the session id (progress->sid) by copying value_len bytes from *data->value with ZVAL_STRINGL. If instead the name equals rfc1867_name (the PHP_SESSION_UPLOAD_PROGRESS field), the prefix and the value (value_len bytes) are appended into progress->key via smart_str, NUL-terminated, and early_find_sid runs. Both copies use value_len, which may originate from the externally set *data->newlength rather than the true buffer size.

FILE_START begins tracking a single uploaded file. It bails unless both a session id and a progress key were established during FORMDATA. As with the FORMDATA guard, this test dereferences progress->sid and progress->key.s directly, so it is only safe if progress is non-NULL.

On the first file, the progress array is built. update_step is either a fixed byte threshold (rfc1867_freq >= 0) or, when negative, a percentage of the total upload: content_length * -freq / 100. It then initializes the 'data' and 'files' arrays and populates data with start_time, content_length, bytes_processed, done and a nested files array. Line 87 caches a pointer INTO the data hash for the 'bytes_processed' slot (progress->post_bytes_processed) so later events can update it in place. It initializes the real session (php_rinit_session), sets PS(id) from the resolved sid, and optionally toggles trans-sid flags. The cached interior pointer is the load-bearing detail: it must remain valid across every subsequent array mutation.

A per-file array is created and populated with field_name, name (from the attacker-supplied filename), an empty tmp_name, error/done flags, start_time, and a bytes_processed slot initialized to 0. It is appended to the files array. Line 113 caches an interior pointer to this file's bytes_processed slot (progress->current_file_bytes_processed), and line 115 immediately writes through it. php_session_rfc1867_update flushes the progress arrays into $_SESSION.

As chunks of a file arrive, FILE_DATA updates the two cached interior pointers with the running byte counts (offset+length for the current file, post_bytes_processed for the whole request). The guard only checks sid/key; it does NOT verify that current_file_bytes_processed / post_bytes_processed were ever set (they are only assigned inside the FILE_START first-time block). Writing through those pointers assumes FILE_START ran and that the arrays they point into have not been reallocated.

FILE_END finalizes one file: records the temp filename, the cancel/error code, marks the file done, and updates the overall post_bytes_processed through the cached interior pointer. Note that add_assoc_string_ex on current_file (line 140) mutates that array AFTER current_file_bytes_processed was cached from it (line 113), which is exactly the kind of mutation that can invalidate an interior hash pointer.

END finalizes the upload. If tracking was active, it either cleans up (removing the $_SESSION entry) or marks progress done and flushes once more. Line 159 SEPARATE_ARRAY(&progress->data) performs a copy-on-write duplication of the data array; line 161 then writes through progress->post_bytes_processed, which was cached (line 87) from the ORIGINAL data hash. It then destroys the data/sid/key members, efree's the progress object, and — importantly — sets both the local progress and PS(rfc1867_progress) to NULL so nothing dereferences the freed block afterward.

After the switch, if the upload was cancelled the function returns FAILURE to abort. This tail is correctly NULL-guarded (progress && progress->cancel_upload), so it is safe even in the END case where progress was just freed and nulled. The design intent is clear here: progress is expected to possibly be NULL, which underscores that the in-switch dereferences without such a guard are the risky spots.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: progress is loaded from PS(rfc1867_progress), which is NULL before MULTIPART_EVENT_START creates the object and after MULTIPART_EVENT_END frees it (line 175). Every consumer branch (FORMDATA line 29 'Z_TYPE(progress->sid)', FILE_START line 63, FILE_DATA line 122, FILE_END line 135, END line 154) dereferences progress with NO prior NULL check — the very guard that is supposed to protect the branch is itself a dereference. If the SAPI multipart parser (or the chained orig callback at line 8, which runs first and can influence state) delivers any non-START event while PS(rfc1867_progress) is NULL — e.g. a FORMDATA/FILE_DATA event without a preceding START, or rfc1867_enabled toggled on mid-parse so START was skipped — the switch dereferences a NULL pointer and crashes the worker (DoS). The whole design assumes 'START always fires exactly once before anything else'; that ordering contract is enforced only by the untrusted request parser, not by this function. The tail check at line 180 ('if (progress && progress->cancel_upload)') proves the authors know progress can legitimately be NULL, yet the in-switch derefs omit the same guard. Invariant: every branch that reads progress-> must first confirm progress != NULL (or the parser must guarantee START-first, which is not defense-in-depth).

zend_hash_str_find returns NULL when the key is absent. Its results (progress->post_bytes_processed at line 87, progress->current_file_bytes_processed at line 113) are dereferenced by Z_LVAL_P without a NULL check at lines 115, 126, 127, 146 and 161. In the immediate first-file path the key was just inserted so the lookup succeeds, but current_file_bytes_processed persists across events: a FILE_DATA (line 126) or FILE_END (line 146) that arrives without the FILE_START first-time block ever having initialized it — or after progress->current_file was rebuilt for a different file — dereferences a NULL or stale cached pointer. Because FILE_DATA/FILE_END only gate on sid/key (lines 122, 135), not on whether the cached slot pointer was set for the current file, an attacker who controls event ordering and file boundaries in the multipart stream can reach a Z_LVAL_P on an unset/NULL pointer. Invariant: never dereference a zend_hash_str_find result without checking it is non-NULL, and never write through a cached interior pointer whose owning array may have been replaced.

progress->post_bytes_processed (line 87) and progress->current_file_bytes_processed (line 113) are raw pointers into a HashTable's element storage. Any operation that reallocates or copies that table invalidates them. Line 159 SEPARATE_ARRAY(&progress->data) forces a copy-on-write duplication of the data array when its refcount > 1 (it is exported into $_SESSION by php_session_rfc1867_update, so it can be shared): after separation progress->data points at a NEW HashTable, but progress->post_bytes_processed still points into the OLD one, and line 161 writes through that dangling pointer (write to freed/old memory, CWE-416/CWE-824). The same hazard applies to add_assoc_string_ex on current_file at line 140 running after current_file_bytes_processed was cached at line 113, and to add_assoc_bool_ex at line 160 which can rehash progress->data. Invariant: interior hash pointers must be re-fetched after any add_assoc_*/SEPARATE_ARRAY on the same array, or the value must be updated by key rather than through a stale cached pointer.

value_len is taken from *data->newlength when a chained callback set it (the comment states this explicitly) and then used to copy value_len bytes out of *data->value: ZVAL_STRINGL(&progress->sid, (*data->value), value_len) at line 45 and smart_str_appendl(&progress->key, *data->value, value_len) at line 49. Neither copy re-validates value_len against the actual allocated length of *data->value. If a preceding filter/callback sets *data->newlength larger than the buffer backing *data->value (or leaves them inconsistent), both copies read past the end of the value buffer (out-of-bounds read, CWE-125) and the over-read bytes become the session id / progress key, i.e. potential information disclosure into $_SESSION. Invariant: value_len must be bounded by the true size of *data->value before any bytes are copied; a downstream length override must never be trusted to exceed the source buffer.

content_length comes straight from the attacker's Content-Length header (line 20). When session.upload_progress.freq is configured as a negative percentage, update_step is computed as content_length * -freq / 100 in signed arithmetic. A very large content_length multiplied by -freq can overflow the signed result (CWE-190), producing a negative or wrapped update_step. That value governs the throttling of php_session_rfc1867_update flushes; a wrapped/negative threshold can cause excessive session writes on every chunk (amplification/DoS) or, depending on the comparison semantics downstream, never/always updating. Invariant: bound content_length (or compute the percentage in a wide unsigned type with an explicit cap) before multiplying so update_step stays a sane non-negative threshold.

summary: Attacker-controlled multipart upload callback whose central risk is a NULL/stale pointer dereference (CWE-476/CWE-416): the progress object and the cached interior hash pointers (post_bytes_processed, current_file_bytes_processed) are dereferenced across events with only sid/key guards, relying entirely on the untrusted parser emitting events in the expected order and on interior pointers surviving array mutation/SEPARATE_ARRAY. Secondary risks are an out-of-bounds read when *data->newlength overrides the value length and a signed overflow computing update_step from the attacker's Content-Length.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Dispatch over an attacker-influenced event with per-case unchecked casts of event_data; the whole flow depends on event ordering.
Constrained by the switch; unknown values fall through harmlessly. Its danger is ordering, captured under the progress-NULL finding.
Passed unchanged to the chained callback (line 8); not dereferenced here.
NULL before START / after END and dereferenced in every consumer branch without a guard (CWE-476).
PHP's emalloc/ecalloc aborts the request on OOM rather than returning NULL, so no unchecked-return NULL deref.
Attacker Content-Length feeds the signed multiply at line 73 (overflow); anchored there.
Length of a server-configured, NUL-terminated string; not attacker-controlled.
Externally overridable length drives byte copies from *data->value without re-bounding (CWE-125).
Copies value_len bytes from the value buffer; consumer of the newlength over-read finding.
Second consumer of value_len; same over-read contract as line 45.
name_len is strlen(data->name); reading name_len+1 includes the NUL and stays within the NUL-terminated buffer.
Signed multiply on attacker Content-Length can overflow (CWE-190).
Unchecked return, cached as an interior hash pointer dereferenced later (CWE-476/CWE-416).
Unchecked/stale interior pointer dereferenced in FILE_DATA/FILE_END without confirming it is set for the current file.
Write through a possibly NULL/stale cached pointer.
Write through cached pointer after array mutation/SEPARATE_ARRAY may hit freed storage (CWE-416).
COW-duplicates the array, invalidating the cached post_bytes_processed pointer used at line 161.
PHP string-copies the attacker name/filename into a zval; no length is computed from it here, and it mutates current_file after the cache — that mutation risk is covered by the stale-pointer finding.
Both aliases nulled immediately after free; correct teardown.
Correctly NULL-guarded; safe even after END frees progress.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference of progress when a non-START event arrives with no live progress object
findings.anchor: progress = PS(rfc1867_progress); then progress-> derefs in every case
findings.bug_class: NULL pointer dereference (missing existence guard on shared state)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0295_cwe476.c (and the SAPI rfc1867 multipart parser, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: Every switch branch that reads progress-> must first verify progress != NULL; the code must not assume the untrusted parser always emits MULTIPART_EVENT_START before any consumer event.
findings.failure_mode: PS(rfc1867_progress) is NULL before START creates it (line 19) and after END frees it (line 175). FORMDATA (line 29), FILE_START (line 63), FILE_DATA (line 122), FILE_END (line 135) and END (line 154) all dereference progress with no NULL check — the guard itself (Z_TYPE(progress->sid)) is the dereference. A crafted multipart stream, an orig_callback that alters flow, or rfc1867_enabled toggled mid-parse so START is skipped, delivers a consumer event while progress is NULL and crashes the worker (remote DoS). The line-180 'if (progress && ...)' guard shows the authors know progress can be NULL, yet the in-switch derefs omit it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the SAPI multipart parser can ever emit FORMDATA/FILE_*/END before START (or after PS(rfc1867_progress) was nulled) for this callback, and whether any per-case 'if (!progress) break;' guard exists in the real build.

findings.title: Unchecked/stale zend_hash_str_find interior pointer dereferenced by Z_LVAL_P
findings.bug_class: NULL/stale pointer dereference (unchecked lookup result cached and written through)
findings.invariant: A zend_hash_str_find result must be NULL-checked before Z_LVAL_P, and a cached interior pointer must not be written through in a later event unless it was (re)initialized for the array instance currently in progress->current_file.
findings.failure_mode: zend_hash_str_find returns NULL for a missing key. progress->post_bytes_processed (line 87) and progress->current_file_bytes_processed (line 113) are cached and later written via Z_LVAL_P at lines 115, 126, 127, 146, 161 with no NULL check. FILE_DATA and FILE_END gate only on sid/key (lines 122, 135), not on whether the cached slot is valid for the current file, so an attacker who reorders/splits multipart events (e.g. FILE_DATA whose current-file array differs from the one cached, or before the first-time init ran) triggers a write through a NULL/stale zval pointer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether FILE_DATA/FILE_END can execute with current_file_bytes_processed/post_bytes_processed pointing at a freed or never-set slot, and whether zend_hash_str_find is guaranteed non-NULL for every reachable path.

findings.title: Write through interior hash pointer invalidated by SEPARATE_ARRAY / array mutation (use-after-free)
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free / write to invalidated pointer (dangling interior HashTable pointer)
findings.invariant: After any operation that can reallocate or copy-on-write the owning array (SEPARATE_ARRAY, add_assoc_* that rehashes), the interior pointer must be re-fetched by key before being dereferenced.
findings.failure_mode: progress->post_bytes_processed (line 87) points into progress->data's element storage. SEPARATE_ARRAY at line 159 duplicates that array when it is shared (it is exported into $_SESSION), so progress->data now owns a NEW HashTable while post_bytes_processed still references the OLD (potentially freed) one; line 161 writes through that dangling pointer. add_assoc_bool_ex at line 160 and add_assoc_string_ex at line 140 can likewise rehash and relocate elements, invalidating the cached pointers used at lines 146/161.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether SEPARATE_ARRAY/add_assoc_* on progress->data can relocate its HashTable while progress->post_bytes_processed still points at the pre-mutation storage in the real Zend build.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read via externally overridden *data->newlength driving value copy
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0295_cwe476.c (and any registered input filter/orig callback, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: value_len must never exceed the allocated length of *data->value; a downstream newlength override must be validated against the source buffer before copying.
findings.failure_mode: Per the line-33 comment, a chained callback may set *data->newlength independently of the buffer backing *data->value. value_len is then used to copy bytes at line 45 (ZVAL_STRINGL into progress->sid) and line 49 (smart_str_appendl into progress->key) with no re-validation. If newlength exceeds the value buffer, both copies over-read heap memory, and the leaked bytes land in the session id / progress key that scripts can read back from $_SESSION (information disclosure).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any input filter can set *data->newlength larger than the allocation behind *data->value, and whether ZVAL_STRINGL/smart_str_appendl copy exactly value_len bytes without an internal bound.

findings.title: Signed integer overflow computing update_step from attacker Content-Length
findings.invariant: content_length must be range-checked (or the percentage computed in a wide unsigned type with a cap) so update_step remains a sane non-negative flush threshold.
findings.failure_mode: content_length is the attacker's declared Content-Length (line 20). When session.upload_progress.freq is a negative percentage, content_length * -freq can overflow the signed result, yielding a wrapped or negative update_step. That governs how often php_session_rfc1867_update writes to the session store; a degenerate threshold causes a session write on every chunk (I/O amplification / DoS) or defeats throttling entirely.
findings.what_to_confirm: The declared type/width of content_length and update_step and whether large Content-Length values overflow the multiply before the divide by 100.

audit_checklist: Add an explicit 'if (!progress) break;' (or equivalent) at the top of every non-START case, and confirm the SAPI parser cannot emit consumer events before START or after PS(rfc1867_progress) is nulled.
NULL-check every zend_hash_str_find result before Z_LVAL_P (lines 87, 113 feeding 115/126/127/146/161).

Re-fetch progress->post_bytes_processed / current_file_bytes_processed by key after SEPARATE_ARRAY (line 159) and any add_assoc_* that can rehash, or update by key instead of through the cached interior pointer.
Bound value_len (lines 35/37) against the true allocation of *data->value before ZVAL_STRINGL (line 45) and smart_str_appendl (line 49); do not trust a callback-set *data->newlength to fit.
Guard the update_step computation (line 73) against signed overflow for large attacker Content-Length.
Verify current_file_bytes_processed cannot be dereferenced in FILE_DATA/FILE_END for a file whose FILE_START first-time init never ran.
